meta.title: Kim's Kitchen — Phở & Spring Rolls
meta.description: Kim's Kitchen — handcrafted Vietnamese phở and fresh spring rolls, made slow and made with love.
hero.title:
Sài Gòn soul,
cooked in Italy.
hero.lede: Kim grew up in Ho Chi Minh City and now cooks out of a kitchen in Italy — phở simmered for sixteen hours, spring rolls wrapped to order, and a quiet love affair with the markets, herbs and slow rhythms of her adopted home. Two cultures, one stove.
dishes.title:
Two dishes.
Done properly.
dishes.intro: Kim keeps the menu small on purpose — every bowl, every roll gets her full attention. The recipes come from her mother's kitchen in Sài Gòn; the herbs, more often than not, come from a balcony garden in Italy.
dish.pho.subtitle: — the soul of a Sài Gòn morning
dish.pho.desc: Beef bones charred over open flame, then coaxed for sixteen hours with ginger, star anise, cassia, cloves and a single black cardamom pod. Served with hand-cut rice noodles, thin-shaved brisket, and a forest of herbs.
dish.rolls.subtitle: — summer wrapped in rice paper
dish.rolls.desc: Translucent rice paper around poached prawn, vermicelli, butter lettuce, mint and Thai basil. Wrapped to order so the paper never gets tacky, paired with house peanut sauce or tangy nước chấm.
story.title:
Three things
Kim refuses to rush.
story.intro: Vietnamese cooking is mostly about restraint — the right ingredient at the right time, and then leaving it alone. Here's what that looks like in her kitchen.
story.step1.p: Marrow bones blister over a flame until aromatic, then drop into water that's only ever allowed to whisper. A rolling boil clouds the broth — Kim won't have it.
story.step2.h: Toasted in a dry pan, by smell.
story.step2.p: Star anise, cassia bark, cloves, coriander seed, fennel — toasted until the kitchen tells her to stop. Bundled in muslin so the broth stays clear and bright.
story.step3.h: Cut at the last possible moment.
story.step3.p: Thai basil, sawtooth coriander, mint, scallions, bird's-eye chili. Picked, washed, torn — never chopped — and added at the table so nothing goes flat.
blend.title:
Where Sài Gòn
meets the Mediterranean.
blend.intro: Kim doesn't fuse the two cuisines — she lets them sit at the same table. Vietnamese technique, Italian pantry. A long simmer is a long simmer in any language.
blend.vn.p: Fish sauce instead of salt. Herbs torn, not chopped. Broth that tells you when it's done. Recipes passed down without measurements — only feel.
blend.it.p: A weekly trip to the market for whatever's best. Piedmontese beef bones for the broth, Ligurian basil when the Thai variety is sleeping, olive oil where it earns its place.
about.title: From a Sài Gòn alleyway to a kitchen in Italy.
about.p1: Kim grew up in Ho Chi Minh City — learning to cook at her mother's elbow in a narrow kitchen that opened straight onto the street, where the smell of charring shallots blended with motorbike exhaust and the neighbour's coffee. She moved to Italy in her twenties for love, and stayed for the food.
about.quote: "Italians and Vietnamese understand the same thing — that a good broth, a good ragù, a good anything takes all day. You can't be in a hurry."
about.p2: Now she cooks somewhere between two grandmothers. Phở made with Piedmontese beef bones. Spring rolls finished with Ligurian basil when Thai basil's out of season. The technique is Vietnamese, but the pantry is half Italian — and somehow it works.
cta.title: Hungry? Kim is cooking this weekend.
cta.body: Drop her a note about what you'd like, how many you're feeding, and when you'd like to pick up. She usually gets back within a day — sooner if the broth isn't on.
foot.tagline: Vietnamese home cooking, made slow — from Sài Gòn, cooked in Italy. Phở and gỏi cuốn to order.
foot.copy: Kim's Kitchen · Made with love & star anise
